BDC Portfolio Stress Test

ⓘ ⓘ ⓘ ⓘ

Global Financial Crisis 2008-09

Calibrated to peak-stress leveraged loan data. This was the worst credit cycle since the Great Depression.

Leveraged loan default rate10.81%S&P/LSTA Index, Nov 2009
1L recovery rate63.4%Moody's Ultimate Recovery Database, 2008 cohort
Loss given default (1L)36.6%= 1 − 63.4%
Realized 1L loss rate~4.0%= 10.81% × 36.6%

Seniority derivation:

1L/DDTL at 4% (direct calculation above). Middle-market loans with financial covenants likely had lower defaults than BSL (Man Group research), offsetting any argument for higher LGD. Revolver at 3% (super-senior priority, higher recovery). 2L at 25% (Moody's 2L recovery expectation was only 21% in June 2008; range of 15-30% is defensible). Equity at 80% (PE buyout deal values dropped 90% from 2006-2009 peak; one in four buyout firms never raised another fund).

JV at 25% (revised down from 40%: a levered 1L vehicle with 4% underlying loss at 1.5x leverage = ~6-10% equity loss; 40% implied 20%+ underlying portfolio losses, inconsistent with the 4% 1L rate). Bond at 20% and Unsecured at 35% (revised from 30/45: the original rates conflated mark-to-market declines with realized losses; Altman data supports ~9% realized HY bond loss, 20% is already conservative).

Sector adjustments:

Consumer +10%, Energy & Materials +10%, Real Estate & Transportation +15% (raised from +8%: CRE was the epicenter of the GFC with values falling 40%+).

Cross-checks:

  • Cliffwater Direct Lending Index (CDLI) 2008 return: −6.50%
  • CDLI peak cumulative realized losses: 9.30% by Q1 2010
  • ARCC NAV decline: −27% ($15.47 → $11.27, SEC 10-K FY2008)
  • ACAS NAV decline: −57% ($18.33 → $7.80; eventually acquired at $3.47)
  • Moody's spec-grade default rate: 13.1% globally (higher than leveraged loan rate)
  • For an 85% 1L portfolio at 1.0x leverage, this scenario produces ~5-7% portfolio loss → ~10-14% NAV decline, consistent with well-managed BDC experience

Sources: S&P Global/LCD Leveraged Loan Default Study; Moody's "Corporate Default & Recovery Rates, 1920-2010"; Moody's Ultimate Recovery Database; Cliffwater 2017 Q1 Report on U.S. Direct Lending; S&P 2020 Annual Global Corporate Default Study (Table 7); ARCC 10-K FY2008 (SEC EDGAR); Man Group "Impact of Scale on Credit Risk"; Lord Abbett CLO Equity Resilience Study.

Dot-Com / Telecom Bust 2001-02

Dominated by telecom and tech sector defaults (WorldCom, Enron, Adelphia, Global Crossing). BDCs did not yet exist; leveraged loan data used as proxy.

Leveraged loan default rate~7%Fed/S&P LCD (Powell 2015 speech)
Spec-grade default rate10.4%Moody's Annual Default Study, 2002
1L recovery (downturn est.)65-70%Moody's LGD calibrated to 2001-02
Loss given default (1L)~32%= 1 − ~68% midpoint
Realized 1L loss rate~2.3%= 7% × 32.5%

Seniority derivation:

1L/DDTL at 2% (calculation above; could be nudged to 2.5% for middle-market uncertainty premium, but 2% is defensible for diversified 1L). 2L at 15% (mezzanine debt had devastating outcomes; ~20% default × ~75% LGD for the most leveraged borrowers). Bond at 20% (revised from 25%: Altman data supports ~10% realized loss on broad HY; 20% is conservative for BDC-quality bonds). Unsecured at 32% (revised from 40%: senior unsecured recovery was only $21.82 in 2002 per Moody's, but 40% implied near-universal default). Equity at 70% (NASDAQ fell 78% peak-to-trough; private equity worse with leverage).

JV at 15% (revised from 35%: at 2% 1L loss with 1.5x JV leverage, equity loss = ~5-8%; 35% was 17.5× the 1L rate, implying 20%+ underlying losses inconsistent with a 2% 1L assumption).

Sector adjustments:

Software & Technology +25%, Media & Telecom +30%. These were the epicenter. WorldCom alone accounted for $46B+ in defaults. Modern BDC "software" is enterprise SaaS with recurring revenue, structurally different from 2001 speculative fiber-optic buildouts, but not immune to sector-wide repricing.

Key caveats:

  • BDCs did not exist during this period — the S&P/LSTA Leveraged Loan Index was young and skewed large-cap
  • The "~7%" leveraged loan figure comes from a Fed speech citing an S&P chart, not a precise tabulation (confidence: medium-high)
  • The 1L downturn recovery of 65-70% is a Moody's forward-looking LGD assessment calibrated to this recession, not a directly observed number
  • HY bond losses were far worse (12.8% default × 74.8% LGD = ~9.6%), but bonds are not comparable to BDC first-lien loans
  • This was a multi-year bust (2001-2003); a single-period model understates cumulative damage (3-year HY default ~55%, per Invesco)

Sources: Moody's "Corporate Default & Recovery Rates, 1920-2006"; Altman & Bana, "Defaults and Returns in the High-Yield Bond Market: The Year 2002 in Review" (NYU Stern/SSRN); Fed Gov. Powell speech, Feb 18 2015; Moody's Ultimate Recovery Database; CFO.com citing Moody's 2008 LGD study.

COVID-19 Crisis 2020

Sharp but short-lived shock. Massive fiscal/monetary intervention (CARES Act, PPP, Fed credit facilities) dramatically reduced realized losses vs. initial forecasts. This scenario models actual outcomes, not counterfactual.

Leveraged loan default rate4.17%S&P/LSTA Index, Sept 2020 (T12M peak)
Moody's spec-grade default rate8.5%Moody's, year-end 2020
1L recovery rate76.1%S&P Global, 2020 default cohort
Loss given default (1L)23.9%= 1 − 76.1%
Realized 1L loss rate~1.0%= 4.17% × 23.9%

Seniority derivation:

1L/DDTL at 1% (direct calculation; could argue 1.5% given 5.1% non-accrual peak, but realized 1L losses were truly ~1%). 2L at 10% (raised from 8% based on GFC proportionality: if GFC 2L was 25% at 4% 1L, COVID 2L should be ~10% at 1% 1L; the recovery gap between 1L and 2L is amplified in stress). Unsecured at 18% (raised from 15%: structural subordination gap was understated). Equity at 40% (PE markdowns were significant in Q1-Q2 but many recovered by year-end).

Sector adjustments:

Consumer +12% (raised from +10%: hospitality, retail, entertainment were devastated; sector-specific defaults were concentrated here). Energy & Materials +15% (oil demand collapse; energy accounted for 28% of all loan defaults).

Cross-checks:

  • CDLI cumulative realized losses: −3.30% (vs. −10.16% in GFC)
  • CDLI 2020 full-year return: +5% (Q1 loss fully reversed)
  • ARCC NAV: −10% peak-to-trough ($17.32 → $15.58, 10-K FY2020)
  • ARCC non-accruals: peaked at 5.1% at cost / 3.2% at FV (Q3 2020)
  • Moody's initial forecast was 12.5% default rate — intervention cut actual to 8.5%

Note: This models actual outcomes with $6T+ in fiscal/monetary support. A "COVID without intervention" scenario would be significantly harsher (closer to GFC levels). Use the custom sliders or the GFC preset to model a hypothetical unmitigated pandemic scenario.

Sources: S&P Global/LCD Default Study; Cliffwater CDLI Q4 2023 Report on U.S. Direct Lending; ARCC 10-K FY2020 (SEC EDGAR); Moody's Q1 2021 Default Report; futurestandard.com "Private Credit Performance in Downturns".

Oil Price Crash / Energy Crisis 2015-16

Sector-concentrated crisis driven by crude oil falling from $100+ to $26/bbl. Defaults concentrated in energy; other sectors were minimally affected.

Broad leveraged loan default~4%S&P/LCD (incl. distressed exchanges)
Energy sector loan default16.6%S&P/LCD, T12M Oct 2016
Energy HY bond default13%Fitch Ratings, by May 2016
Broad loan recovery~70%PitchBook (2015-2021 avg)
Realized 1L loss (non-energy)~1%= ~4% × 30% LGD (non-energy)

Seniority derivation:

Base seniority rates are zeroed for senior debt (1L, Unitranche, DDTL, Revolver, ABF all at 0%) because this was a sector-concentrated crisis — non-energy portfolios were essentially unaffected. Subordinated base rates are modest (2L 2%, Equity 10%) to capture mild broad-market pressure. The Energy & Materials +10% sector overlay carries the story.

Recalibration note: The sector add-on was reduced from +40% to +10%. The multiplicative model (combined = 1−(1−sen)(1−sec)) previously created a 40.6% energy 1L loss, roughly 7× the actual ~6% realized loss (16.6% default × ~35% LGD). At +10%, energy 1L = 10% combined — still somewhat high but within range for the worst-exposed BDCs. The model architecture cannot simultaneously represent low energy 1L losses (~6%) and high energy equity losses (~50%+) with a single sector multiplier; it is calibrated for overall portfolio accuracy.

Cross-checks:

  • CDLI cumulative realized losses: −1.86% (mild for diversified portfolios)
  • CDLI income exceeded losses by +8.44%
  • 100+ energy bankruptcies (Fitch), 30 energy loan issuers defaulted
  • Energy HY OAS reached 1,415 bps (vs. 335 bps in mid-2014)
  • Non-energy BDCs (ARCC, MAIN, GBDC) were largely unaffected
  • Energy-heavy BDCs (PSEC, FSK) had material NAV declines

Portfolio impact: For a zero-energy portfolio, this preset is equivalent to Reset (senior debt at 0%). For a portfolio with 10% energy exposure, expect ~1-2% total portfolio loss, consistent with the CDLI -1.86% cumulative figure.

Sources: S&P Global Market Intelligence/LCD; Fitch Ratings (Nov 2016, BusinessWire); Cliffwater CDLI Q4 2023 Report; PitchBook "Out-of-court restructurings lift leveraged loan recovery rates"; FRED BAMLH0A0HYM2 (ICE BofA HY OAS).

Seniority Total Loss Rates ⓘ

Two independent risk dimensions. Seniority rates reflect capital stack position (structural protection). Sector rates reflect cyclical/macro exposure. They combine multiplicatively:

Combined = 1 − (1 − seniority) × (1 − sector)

Example: 5% seniority + 10% sector = 14.5% combined loss — not 15%. This avoids double-counting since the two dimensions are independent.

Total Loss Rate = Default Rate × Loss Given Default

Sector Total Loss Rates

Total Loss Rate = Default Rate × Loss Given Default

ⓘ
years

Loans maturing within the repricing window are excluded from stress losses and treated as cash recovered at par.

The assumption: maturing loans get repaid and redeployed at efficiently priced terms given current market awareness. Only loans maturing after the window are subject to the modeled loss rates.

Positions without a maturity date (equity, preferred, JV) are always fully exposed regardless of this setting.

ⓘ
Non-accrual loss floor %
PIK-accruing loss floor %

Non-accrual loss floor — minimum loss rate applied to every non-accrual position regardless of seniority/sector. A non-accrual loan is one the issuer has stopped paying interest on; the BDC has already conceded that recovery is impaired.

PIK-accruing loss floor — minimum loss rate applied to every PIK (payment-in-kind) position that is not already non-accrual. PIK is interest accrued as additional principal instead of cash; it is often (but not always) a signal of borrower distress.

Both sliders operate as a floor. If your base sector×seniority loss for a cell is already higher than the slider, the slider has no effect. Otherwise the slider rate replaces it for the impaired slice only.

JAOD Extreme sets NA = 100% (every non-accrual fully written off) and PIK = 50%.

Loss by Seniority

Loss by Sector

Capital Structure Waterfall ⓘ

How losses flow through the capital stack. Investments are split into encumbered (pledged to secured credit facilities) and unencumbered (free) pools.

Secured lenders claim encumbered assets first. Excess encumbered assets flow to the general pool. If encumbered assets fall short, the secured shortfall becomes pari passu with unsecured noteholders against unencumbered assets.

Unsecured recovery shows cents on the dollar available to unsecured bondholders after all senior claims are satisfied.

Debt amounts use carrying value where filings provide it, otherwise face value. Instrument amounts are never adjusted—any gap between summed instruments and the balance sheet total (from deferred financing costs, unamortized discounts) appears as an explicit “Accounting Adj.” line with its own breakdown.

Encumbered/unencumbered splits from iXBRL footnote data. Losses applied proportionally across both pools. Debt at carrying value where available.

Encumbrance assumption. We conservatively estimate that all assets marked as “encumbered” or “pledged” in SEC filings are fully encumbered—i.e., 100% of their fair value is pledged to secured credit facilities.

This is likely an overstatement. In practice, credit facility borrowing bases typically apply advance rates (e.g., 50–70% of fair value), meaning a significant portion of “encumbered” assets may have residual unencumbered value available to unsecured creditors. The true encumbered fraction is almost certainly lower than shown.

Balance sheet reconciliation. This line reconciles the waterfall to reported equity. Two sources of difference:

Deferred financing costs & unamortized discounts. Debt instruments are shown at carrying value where filings provide it, otherwise at face/outstanding value. The balance sheet “total debt” line is net of all deferred financing costs and unamortized discounts, so the instrument-level sum can exceed it. This is not missing debt—it’s a GAAP presentation difference. Common on CLO structures and credit facilities where issuance costs are material.

SOI rounding. Summing individual position fair values from the Schedule of Investments can differ from the balance sheet “total investments at fair value” by small amounts due to rounding across hundreds of positions.

Click the row to see the breakdown. These adjustments ensure the waterfall reproduces reported NAV at zero stress without distorting any instrument amount or debt priority.

Portfolio FV ⓘ
--

Net funded fair value. All portfolio values are net of unfunded commitments (undrawn revolvers, delayed draw term loans, equity commitments) and marked to current fair value.

This matches each BDC's "Total Investments at Fair Value" as reported in their SEC filings, after subtracting unfunded commitment line items from the Schedule of Investments.

Total Loss
--
Loss %
--
Post-Stress FV
--
Implied NAV/Share ⓘ
--

Post-stress NAV per share. Portfolio losses flow through leverage directly to equity. Equity residual = total assets − portfolio loss − all liabilities. Implied NAV/share = equity residual / shares outstanding.

The leverage multiplier means a 10% portfolio loss can translate to a 20-30% equity loss depending on the debt/equity ratio.

positions · Stress values reflect current slider settings
Name ▲ Tranche Fair Value MTM % Stress Value Stress Loss Sector Encumbered Spread PIK Non-Accrual

Actual bond trading data from FINRA TRACE (via Public.com / TradingView, T+1 close). Prices and yields-to-maturity are real last-trade data, not model estimates.

Credit spread = market YTM − maturity-matched treasury rate. Treasury curve interpolated from FRED (2yr/5yr/10yr, fetched live).

X-axis metrics explained:

Debt/Equity LeverageTotal debt / equity. Higher = more levered.
Equity CushionEquity / total assets. How far assets can fall before any debt is impaired. BDC regulatory min is ~33% (150% asset coverage).
Asset Coverage: Total DebtTotal assets / total borrowings. BDC regulatory minimum is 150%. This is the standard 1940 Act coverage test.
Coverage: All LiabilitiesTotal assets / total liabilities (debt + other liabilities like payables, deferred income). Stricter than the debt-only coverage.
Unsecured Bond Coverage(Total assets − secured debt − other liabilities) / unsecured debt. The pool available to unsecured bondholders after senior claims. This is what you care about as an unsecured bondholder.
Unsecured Bond Recovery %Stressed version of unsecured coverage, capped at 100%. The actual cents-on-the-dollar unsecured bondholders would receive in the stress scenario.

Spreads vary significantly by issuer: FSK (BB+) trades 100-120bps wide of ARCC (BBB). OBDC trades 50-70bps wide of ARCC at similar maturities despite similar ratings.

Data sources: Bond prices/YTM from FINRA TRACE via Public.com & TradingView. Treasury curve from FRED. CUSIPs from Public.com URL slugs & OBDC IR page.

No CUSIP available. This bond was identified from SEC filings (coupon, maturity, outstanding amount) but we don't have a confirmed CUSIP identifier for it.

Without a CUSIP, we can't look up the actual TRACE trade price. The YTM and spread shown are interpolated from the issuer's verified spread curve (built from bonds that do have CUSIPs and TRACE data).

CUSIPs are sourced from Public.com URL slugs and issuer IR pages. If you have the CUSIP for this bond, we can add verified TRACE pricing.

BDC Bond Market ⓘ

Chart: vs

Historical PIK Exposure ⓘ

PIK (Payment-in-Kind) income is interest or dividends earned but not received in cash — instead the amount is added to the principal balance.

% of GII: Quarterly PIK income as a percentage of Gross Investment Income (from SEC EDGAR XBRL).

% of FV: Fair value of positions with PIK toggled on as a percentage of total portfolio fair value (from iXBRL Schedule of Investments).

Historical Non-Accrual ⓘ

Non-accrual fair value from SEC EDGAR XBRL filings (where reported). Non-accrual status means the BDC has stopped recognizing income on the loan.

Left axis: non-accrual fair value in dollars. Right axis: non-accrual as % of total portfolio fair value. Only BDCs that report this metric in XBRL are shown (currently GBDC).

Quarterly Comparison

Current PIK Exposure ⓘ

Payment-in-Kind (PIK). Instead of paying cash interest, the borrower adds accrued interest to the loan principal. PIK increases exposure over time without generating cash flow.

High PIK concentration signals credit stress — borrowers that can't service cash interest. PIK positions also grow the portfolio's funded exposure, increasing loss severity in a downturn.

Current Non-Accrual Exposure ⓘ

Non-accrual means the BDC has stopped recognizing interest income on the loan, typically because the borrower has missed payments or is expected to default.

Non-accrual status is disclosed via footnotes in the Schedule of Investments. These positions are already marked down (fair value < cost) and represent the portfolio's most distressed credits.

ⓘ

Position-level (default): Only the specific loan tranches flagged as non-accrual in the filing are counted. This matches the filing's reported non-accrual percentage most closely.

Company-level: When enabled, ALL positions of a company are counted as non-accrual if ANY of its tranches are on non-accrual. This gives a more conservative view of total exposure to distressed borrowers, including performing senior debt at the same company.

▶ Liquidity Assumptions

Borrower-Drawable Draw Rate ⓘ%
BDC-Contingent Draw Rate ⓘ%

Borrower-Drawable Unfunded — % of revolver and delayed-draw term-loan commitments assumed to be drawn by borrowers during stress. In a downturn, distressed borrowers max out credit lines, forcing the BDC to fund new loans at par into a declining portfolio.

BDC-Contingent Unfunded — % of BDC-controlled equity or JV commitments (SDLP, COPJV, portfolio-company equity) assumed to be funded. These are not borrower-initiated draws — the BDC typically funds these only when deals close or JV capital calls occur. Default 10% reflects that contingent events are materially less likely than borrower-driven revolver draws.

Forced Sale Disc. ⓘ%

Forced Sale Discount — haircut applied if assets must be liquidated quickly to raise cash.

Middle-market loans are illiquid. In stress, forced sellers receive well below fair value. A 15% discount means $1B of assets yields only $850M in cash — the liquidity premium for fire-sale conditions.

Dividend Rate /qtr ⓘ%
Revolver Capacity Haircut ⓘ%

Dividend Rate — quarterly dividend as % of post-stress equity (annualized = 4× this rate).

BDCs must distribute 90%+ of taxable income to maintain RIC tax status. This is a mandatory cash drain that can't easily be stopped even in stress. The rate is applied to post-stress equity, so larger portfolio losses automatically reduce the dividend burden.

Revolver Capacity Haircut — stress assumption for how much of the BDC's undrawn revolver capacity becomes unavailable in a liquidity crisis (lender covenant breaches, counterparty risk).

▶ Defaults Control (timing split — stress matrix drives loss magnitude)

Total Loss Rate (from stress matrix): 0.00%
Default Rate ⓘ%

Default Rate — portfolio-implied default rate over the stress horizon.

Relationship: Default Rate × (1 − Recovery) = Total Loss Rate (fixed by the stress-tab matrix). Moving this slider auto-adjusts Recovery so total loss stays invariant with the stress model.

Recovery Given Default ⓘ%

Recovery Given Default — cents on the dollar recovered after default (1 − LGD). Higher recovery = more cash comes back at the workout year, fewer loans need to default to produce the same total loss.

Moving this slider auto-adjusts Default Rate to keep the total loss rate (from the stress matrix) constant.

Time to Work Out ⓘmo

Time to Work Out — months between default and realized recovery. Controls how long cash is tied up in defaulted positions. Recovery cash lands as a lump sum in year (horizon start + ceil(months/12)).

Defaulted loans pay no interest during workout; only the surviving share continues to pay cash interest through maturity.

5-Year Cumulative Liquidity Projection ⓘ

Left = cash needs Right = cash available

Cumulative inflows vs outflows over the 5-year projection horizon (2026-2030). Each year's bar includes all prior years.

Stress matrix (sector × seniority) drives the loss magnitude and is shared with the Stress Model tab. The Liquidity tab's Defaults Control then decomposes that loss into timing components:

  • Surviving share pays cash interest + principal at scheduled maturity.
  • Defaulted share stops paying interest immediately; a portion returns as a Defaulted-Loan Recoveries lump at workout-year (horizon + ceil(months/12)).
  • Permanent loss = combined × FV, identical to the stress tab.

CLO principal repayment is excluded (non-recourse, match-funded), but CLO interest expense is included as a cash outflow. PIK income is tracked but not counted as a cash inflow. Dividend rate is adjustable above (default 5%/qtr of equity).

Debt Maturity Wall

Asset Coverage Ratio (Post-Stress) ⓘ

Asset coverage ratio = total assets / total borrowings. BDCs must maintain at least 150% by regulation (200% prior to 2019). Most credit facilities impose 200% covenants.

Falling below 150% triggers regulatory action. Below 200% may trigger covenant defaults, accelerating debt repayment and potentially forcing asset sales at distressed prices.

Red line = 150% regulatory minimum. Amber = 200% typical covenant.

Spread Distribution (BPS)

Spread over reference rate (SOFR, EURIBOR, SONIA). Debt positions only.

Duration × Spread (DtS) — Carry vs Risk

X = Spread Duration (annuity factor at all-in yield). Y = Spread (bps). Bubble size = FV. Higher DtS = more carry per unit of spread risk.
